summary: 12th-century French Scholastic philosopher, author of Sic et Non and the autobiographical Historia Calamitatum. Known for his love affair with Heloise, his nominalism/conceptualism about universals, his intentionalist ethics, and his disputes with Bernard of Clairvaux.

works.name: Sic et Non and Dialectical Method
works.description: Abelard's most frequently identified work, Sic et Non ('Yes and No'), juxtaposes 158 contradictory theological statements from Church fathers as an exercise in applying logic to theology. It heavily influenced Peter Lombard's Four Books of Sentences, the standard medieval theology textbook. Abelard's dialectical method of alternating between opposing theses to find common truth was widely copied.

clues.clue: Sic et Non juxtaposes 158 contradicting statements by Church fathers
examples: a book that juxtaposes 158 contradicting statements by Church fathers
assembled citations from opposing patristic authorities on 158 questions

clues.clue: Peter Lombard's Four Books of Sentences systematized the approach laid out in Sic et Non
examples: Peter Lombard's Four Books of Sentences systematize an approach that this philosopher laid out
Abelard's exercise book Sic et Non heavily influenced this work, divided into 'distinctions' by Alexander of Hales

clues.clue: Questions include 'Must human faith be completed by reason, or not?'
examples: The questions of whether faith must be completed by reason and whether faith only deals with things unseen is explored in one book by this thinker.
compiled 158 questions such as 'Must human faith be completed by reason, or not?'

clues.clue: Dialectica was his last logical treatise; also wrote Glosses on Porphyry
examples: This philosopher's views on logic are presented in his Glosses on Porphyry and a treatise called the Dialectica.
his own works include three logical treatises

works.name: Heloise and Historia Calamitatum
works.description: Abelard's autobiographical Historia Calamitatum ('History of My Calamities') recounts his love affair with Heloise, the birth of their son Astrolabe, and his castration by Heloise's uncle Fulbert. Their letters were the first full vernacular translation by Jean de Meun, who recounted their story in the Roman de la Rose. The relationship inspired Rousseau's Julie, or the New Heloise.

clues.clue: Love affair with Heloise; castrated by her uncle Fulbert
examples: name this medieval French author of Sic et non who had an affair with Heloise
Fulbert's men castrated this author of Sic et Non because of his relationship with Heloise

clues.clue: Historia Calamitatum describes the birth of their son Astrolabe and is addressed to Philintus
examples: This philosopher described the birth of Astrolabe in the letter Historia Calamitatum.
The first of these texts is addressed to Philintus and recounts the birth of a boy named Astrolabe

clues.clue: Letters divided into four 'personal' and three 'of direction'; Jean de Meun made first vernacular translation
examples: These texts are traditionally divided into four labelled 'personal' and three 'of direction.' The first full vernacular translation of these texts was made by Jean de Meun

clues.clue: Over 100 fragments discovered in 15th-century monastic source, published by Constant Mews
examples: Following their discovery in a 15th-century monastic source, over 100 fragments of these texts were published by Constant Mews.

clues.clue: Peter Dronke's 1984 'Critical Study' deemed authorship questions 'purest prejudice'
examples: Questions surrounding the authorship of some of these texts were deemed a product of 'purest prejudice' in an excursus to a 1984 'Critical Study' by Peter Dronke.

clues.clue: Heloise argued marriage was 'fatal to a philosopher'; better to be mistress than wife
examples: this thinker recounted how his wife argued marriage was 'fatal to a philosopher' since Socrates 'quitted life with joy because by that means he left Xanthippe.'
this philosopher recounted a woman's argument that it's better to be a mistress than a wife

clues.clue: Heloise addressed 42 questions about scripture to Abelard while living at the Paraclete
examples: that woman addressed 42 questions about scripture to this man while living at the Paraclete

clues.clue: Rousseau used the story as basis for Julie, or the New Heloise
examples: This later philosopher used Abelard's story as the basis of his epistolary novel, Julie, or, The New Heloise.

works.name: Nominalism and the Problem of Universals
works.description: Abelard is often called the first nominalist philosopher, arguing that universals cannot exist in the world (as they would need to be simultaneously rational and irrational) and that 'being a man' is a linguistic concept, not a physical or metaphysical universal. He argued that Boethius's criteria for universals cannot be satisfied by any real object. Despite using the term nomina, he is often credited as the originator of conceptualism.

clues.clue: Argued universals cannot be in the world, as they would simultaneously be rational and irrational; 'being a man' is merely linguistic
examples: this 12th-century nominalist argued that universals could not be in the world, as they would simultaneously be rational and irrational
To argue against essential realism, this philosopher stated that 'being a man' is a linguistic concept

clues.clue: Argued no real object can satisfy Boethius's criteria, so ontological realism about universals is incoherent
examples: This thinker argued that no real object can satisfy Boethius's criteria, and therefore ontological realism about universals is incoherent.

clues.clue: First nominalist; often credited as originator of conceptualism (universals exist only in the mind)
examples: this French author of Sic et Non and the first nominalist philosopher
Abelard is often credited as the originator of this theory separate from nominalism, which holds that universals exist only in the mind.

clues.clue: Teacher was Roscelin (extreme nominalist condemned for tritheism); also studied under William of Champeaux
examples: This man's teacher Roscelin promoted an extreme nominalism that led to his being condemned for believing in three gods.

works.name: Ethics and Intentionalism
works.description: In his Ethics, or Know Thyself, Abelard developed an intentionalist theory according to which actions themselves are morally neutral; only intentions determine moral worth. He rejected the Slave's Wager (an early form of Pascal's Wager) and illustrated his ethics with the thought experiment of two house-builders. Against Anselm's satisfaction theory, he claimed Christ's death was to exemplify perfect love.

clues.clue: Developed an intentionalist ethics in Ethics, or Know Thyself: actions are morally neutral, only intentions matter
examples: This philosopher developed an intentionalist theory according to which actions themselves are all morally neutral in Ethics, or Know Thyself.
A thought experiment in which one man wants to build a house for the poor but lacks the means while another has the desire and the means demonstrates this thinker's intention-based ethics.

clues.clue: Rejected early version of Pascal's Wager called the 'Slave's Wager'
examples: This thinker rejected ethics as conformity to law by refuting an early version of Pascal's Wager called the 'Slave's Wager.'

clues.clue: Responded to teacher's claim about sex in Eden by arguing a monk forced to sleep with women experiences pleasure without sinning
examples: This philosopher's teacher claimed that sex in Eden was like 'putting your finger in your mouth,' prompting this man's response that a monk who is chained up and forced to sleep with two women will experience pleasure without sinning.

clues.clue: Against Anselm's Cur Deus Homo, claimed Christ's death was to exemplify perfect love
examples: Against the satisfaction theory of St. Anselm's Cur Deus Homo?, this man claimed that the role of the Christ's death was to exemplify perfect love.

works.name: Bernard of Clairvaux and Condemnation
works.description: Abelard's contention that reason has a role in interpreting faith angered 'anti-dialecticians' such as Bernard of Clairvaux, who had nineteen of Abelard's propositions condemned at the Council of Sens (1140/1141). Despite this, Abelard is noted for stating that one should not rashly pass judgment on contradictory church fathers.

clues.clue: Bernard of Clairvaux had nineteen of Abelard's propositions condemned at the Council of Sens
examples: Bernard of Clairvaux, who had nineteen of this philosopher's propositions condemned at the Council of Siens
Bernard persuaded Innocent II to convene the 1140 council of Sens to condemn the doctrine of this theologian

clues.clue: Abelard's contention that reason has a role in interpreting faith angered 'anti-dialecticians'
examples: His contention that reason has a role to play in interpreting faith angered 'anti-dialecticians' such as Bernard of Clairvaux

clues.clue: Founded the Oratory of the Paraclete
examples: This founder of the Oratory of the Paraclete assembled citations from opposing patristic authorities

comprehensive_summary:
Peter Abelard is a 12th-century French Scholastic philosopher, most commonly identified as the author of Sic et Non and the lover of Heloise. Sic et Non ('Yes and No') juxtaposes 158 contradictory theological statements from Church fathers as an exercise in applying dialectical logic to theology. The work heavily influenced Peter Lombard's Four Books of Sentences, which became the standard medieval theology textbook. Abelard's dialectical method of alternating between opposing theses was widely copied.

Abelard is equally famous for his tragic love affair with Heloise, recounted in his autobiographical Historia Calamitatum. The affair produced a son named Astrolabe and ended when Heloise's uncle Fulbert had Abelard castrated. Their letters were traditionally divided into four 'personal' and three 'of direction,' with Jean de Meun producing the first vernacular translation and recounting their context in the Roman de la Rose. Heloise argued that marriage was 'fatal to a philosopher' and that it was better to be a mistress than a wife. She later addressed 42 questions about scripture to Abelard while living at the Paraclete, the oratory he founded. Rousseau used their story as the basis for his novel Julie, or the New Heloise.

Philosophically, Abelard is considered the first nominalist, arguing that universals cannot exist in the world because they would simultaneously be rational and irrational, and that 'being a man' is merely a linguistic concept. He argued that Boethius's criteria for universals cannot be satisfied by any real object. Despite using the term nomina, scholars often credit him as the originator of conceptualism, which holds that universals exist only in the mind. His teacher Roscelin promoted an extreme nominalism condemned for tritheism.

In ethics, Abelard developed an intentionalist theory in Ethics, or Know Thyself, arguing that actions themselves are morally neutral and only intentions determine moral worth. He rejected an early version of Pascal's Wager (the 'Slave's Wager') and against Anselm's satisfaction theory, claimed that Christ's death was to exemplify perfect love.

Abelard's contention that reason plays a role in interpreting faith angered Bernard of Clairvaux, who had nineteen of Abelard's propositions condemned at the Council of Sens (1140/1141). His biographer John Marenbon cited Abelard and his student John of Salisbury as advocates for the virtuosity of ancient pagans.
